speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the handwerker barrack, were you aware of any of the transports that were coming in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we knew that's coming-- coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard-- it's rumors-- just rumors, you know? The same thing that the-- I think the last group what we heard came in as-- from-- from somewhere, from Bialystok, from somewhere, they came in. And then was the uprising in the other ghetto-- in the other camp, you see, in Treblinka. When-- after the uprising was completely destroyed, the walls, the gates, and everything, then they-- they hanged around with some clothing to make that-- you see a few passing by, you should not see what's going on inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what you knew about the uprising, while it was happening. How did you know about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We know from them when we saw them running away. And we heard-- we say-- we saw some fire, some smoke coming there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in '42-- when I came into that camp. That's in '43, I think. Yeah, that's in '43, when I came to that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were working that time. We were outside. And-- and as soon as the-- you know, when we saw them running. We saw the people running-- run to the-- the only place where they had to run is only to that forest. You see? This was the only place where they-- they ran away from Treblinka, after going that-- if not, you are on the open field. The open field, they would see you. They would-- So our-- you see, our camp was facing that-- that that forest, you know? But we saw them running. And then-- how far it is from this Treblinka to this Treblinka? It was about-- not even a kilometer. And what is a kilometer? [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard people coming in and telling us-- not telling us, heard from the-- from the-- the Polish barrack, you know, the-- the-- they had more news about everything than we heard the news. So we found it out from them that there was an uprising.

speaker: interviewer
text: And since the uprising, they didn't-- I think they brought in another transport after the uprising. If I'm not mistaken, there was another transport bringing in-- oh, but they had-- didn't have the-- the-- the-- the facilities already to gas them, so the-- they-- they-- they shot them. And-- and-- you know? And the-- the machine was working very-- you heard it, when you were [INAUDIBLE]. Until today, I cannot take that smell from-- from a steak. I cannot even tell-- I hate-- I don't like a steak, because the burning from that meat, from the people, are the sell-- same smell than from the steak. So when I came back here to the United States, when they gave me a steak, I didn't want to eat the steak. I had that feeling, you see? So you heard, constantly, [MACHINE SOUND] the machine was working. [MACHINE SOUND] The motor?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] and taking out and [INAUDIBLE]. It's thousands, and thousands, and thousands of people were there, murdered.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out-- how did you find out what was happening in the other camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found it out before I came to that camp. Before I went to Treblinka, I found out from the Warsaw Ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. But you-- you didn't know the details of what they were doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [STAMMERING] Yeah, I didn't know that-- that-- then, I didn't believe it. It's not a question about I didn't know it-- know it, because people came back-- you know, they came back from there, from that place where they-- they assaulted them, shot them, and burned them, and put them in-- in the graves, in the-- not what-- people was in machines, you see? They dig them and put them in-- in the machine, the-- Like he was-- I-- I-- I saw-- I saw recently, from Bosnia, the-- the-- you know, [STAMMERING] grabbing the machine, grabbing the-- all the deads and putting them in a grave. That's what's-- that's what's happened there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the people in your camp-- what was the reaction, when they learned about the uprising in the other camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me tell you that our camp was also prepared for an uprising. We organized themself. They were-- the day-- the day when they liquidated-- liquidated our camp, the day when they took out everybody to be shot, this morning supposed to be an uprising in our camp. I don't know. There was  a rumor. Then, there was one guy there, which he tipped off the SS that the Jews, they're going to make an uprising there. There was a rumor-- the same day was that the rumor, because in the same day, they liquidated the ghetto. I don't know. It's because the Russian Army, maybe, advanced. [STAMMERING] The rumor was then [STAMMERING] one guy-- we knew that guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said it's Froim Rikner. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [STAMMERS] the other guy, this was not [STAMMERS] they accused him not the right way, because this is Ignac. He was-- he was a kapo there. So he did not do the-- as a matter of fact, they pointed at him also that he was also from the organizers. And they took him out, and they shot him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, Rikner, he-- after the liquidation from the ghetto, from that camp, from Treblinka, where we-- where I was-- I'm not talking about the Treblinka from there-- so that SS, I heard, I found out from one guy, which he was also in Treblinka, and he also ran away. Then he-- they took him with him-- with them when they ran away from the Treblinka. The SS took him with them-- with them by-- alive. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And where he disappeared-- a  matter of fact, after the-- I-- I noticed somewhere, 10, 20 years back, you know, in the paper, that they caught Rikner. They're looking for Rikner, or something. This was it. Oh, they tipped them off. We had-- we're supposed to have-- really, we were supposed to have an uprising in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about the preparations, the planning, for that uprising?

speaker: interviewee
text: They quiet. It's not public. There's no meeting. [HOLDS HAND TO EAR AS IF LISTENING] [NON-ENGLISH] It-- it's the way-- [STAMMERING]

words.text: [HOLDS HAND TO EAR AS IF LISTENING]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know? What were you told?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- you see, they told we should get-- go out and kill that guy what he stays by the gate.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you going to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He walked around, you know? You-- you--

speaker: interviewer
text: With what?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the hand. They had some guns. There was some guns, because-- because some of the prisoners that worked, they cleaned some guns. And he stole. And there was-- was-- was some guns there. But that's-- it was too late already, too late in the game, you see? [INAUDIBLE]. And then, they-- they came that morning. This was the 18th of-- the 20-- I-- I-- I still-- you know, I'm fighting with-- with the other guy, which he's also alive in there. He said a day later, and I say a day earlier. It comes out mine estimation was the 22nd of July, 1944. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: They came. [INAUDIBLE] start to liquidate the [INAUDIBLE] camp that I am-- I was there, you know? How-- what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went in the morning to work, at the normal morning, on a normal Sunday morning. Usually, it was not a-- a normal day, because, most of the time, Sunday, we did not work. Sunday, we had the free time for themselves, you see, to make the beds, to clean up, to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, but this morning, this Sunday morning, was an exceptional, because they took us down to the shop to work. For everybody went to work, you know? And we come. About 12 o'clock, we went out. We came for-- for the-- you know, to the-- they gave us lunch, you know, a little soup, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, we went-- we went back to the shop. About 4 o'clock, you heard the camp is full with Ukrainians, SS, with dogs, with them. Oh, then we knew that something is going to happen. We knew that, because we had that feeling that they're going to liquidate the ghetto. And they came and they did it, to everyone, wherever he was-- [NON-ENGLISH]! Fall! Fall down! Fall down! In the shop and all over, all over, the people were laying on the floor. And I was in the front of that shop where  I came out. And they fell down. I fell down, also. And I was laying on the-- As I was laying there-- well, half an hour, an hour, laid there, the SS started to-- to come around. And they started to take out people. So they came over our group. [INAUDIBLE] I was laying in front of the barrack. I was laying with another group of people. And they picked up two to three the carpenters. They had to finish some work, which I know the-- the-- the carpenters, which they were. Matter of fact, he was living-- he was in Israel also.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took him out. And then they said, [GERMAN] [GERMAN]. I was thinking, what the heck? What? Am I going to lay down on the floor in the-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I stood up with another 19, 20 people. We stood up, and we started to walk from the barrack to the gate, from that place where they-- as we're coming over to the gate, the [GERMAN SPEECH] with the unterhosen.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us-- we should let-- let down the-- the pants, for the-- and start-- we couldn't walk. We hopped. With our hands up and hopped, as if we are hopping. I said to the guys with-- we were 20-- 20 people, five-- five times to four. And from this side, a few Ukrainian, this side, and front, an SS man, and the back, an SS man. [STAMMERS] That's the way, how they [INAUDIBLE] to the grave. And taking us to the grave. And that's how we're walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said to the guys, what we're going to do? We're going to go over there to be shot? I said it quiet, in that quiet way. And I tried to let down my hand, one hand. I said, I'm-- I'm coming, and I want to grab the pants, I-- Get back up. A knock with the rifle. I thought I'm-- I'm dying, right away, on that place. And I-- you know? I-- I-- I lost my conscious. I lost-- I didn't know what is going on. And I-- I-- I picked up my hands, and I hopped again. And I see them coming over close to that-- to that grave. I digged out that grave on my-- I said to myself, Simon, what are you going to-- what are you going to do? You're going to put in your head, they should shot you there? Run! Then you wouldn't feel when you get killed. That's all right. As I'm talking to myself, so quiet, I grabbed my pants, and I started to run. I started to run, feel the first bullet here. You see? [POINTS] The marks is-- is still here. [POINTING] And I-- and I fell down. I-- I couldn't hold up my pants. I fell down, and I'm laying. Two minutes, three minutes later, an SS man, a Ukrainian, came over to me. He looked at me. Are you alive? You son of a this, this, and this. You know? You're alive. And he took that rifle and put it right over here. I have these marks here, you see? [POINTS] He took that rifle and aimed it. And then, bingo, he shot. And it looks like the bullet went down here and didn't hit that nerve. [STAMMERS] And-- and I laid down, and the blood started to run. And I'm laying. In a minute, two minutes later, I hear-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] [MAKES MACHINE GUN NOISES] [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] He shot them. And then, nine minutes later, and I felt the drop after drop of blood coming out. And I-- I'm afraid to move in that place, because if I'm going to move in that place, then somebody will notice that. And them guys are going to shot me. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: In front of me, what-- this looks like-- then somebody else was running with me also, because I heard, in the back of me, one is laying, [MOANING] And it got quieter, quieter. It looks like he was shot with that running. And he died in that place. And I was laying there. I was laying about two-- more than two hours-- more than two hours, until they finished all the people there. This was about 700 people. That's not just 20 people to go out and shot them. And they were-- took in group [INAUDIBLE], groups-- groups. Then, after this group-- after our group, you know-- there was nine people. I find out from the other one, they started to take them out, you know, more then-- more watched-- they watched them more.

speaker: interviewer
text: More carefully?

speaker: interviewee
text: More closer, they should not run. So-- and I was laying, and laying, and laying, until I heard the stop-- the shooting stopped. I knew their psychology. I knew it. If-- now, a job like this, they have to sit down and drink. They-- they have to-- they have to sit down and drink.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's quiet down. And I'm laying about 15, 20 minutes. So I don't hear any more [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] And I didn't hear any more the screaming. I-- SS soldiers are being-- it was a soldier-- I started to crawl on the-- on the ground. I didn't stood up and go along, I had to crawl over to the forest, because the forest was-- I was shot, you know, about 200 feet away from the forest. If I were to go another 200 feet, I wouldn't-- so I crawled and crawled. And finally, when I came over to the beginning of the forest, I fell on-- on-- on a tree. You know? I fell down, and I started to breathe. I started to breathe. And I feel that I'm-- I'm full of blood, you know? I-- what shall I do? What shall I do? I cannot do anything. So I, with one hand, the hand-- so I pulled out that shirt and hold it-- with this hand, I couldn't move anything-- and ripped up a piece, and tied under my-- my neck, and a piece here on the hand. And-- and I started to walk. And I walked about 5, 10 feet away from that [INAUDIBLE]. I came on that-- to that grave where the people were laying-- laying shot. So you heard them-- [GURGLING SOUND] just-- they did-- there was not-- some of them, they were still alive. Some of them, they was you know, working hard until he's died. So he like-- like-- a-- a pot of potatoes would cook. [GURGLING SOUND] [STAMMERS] But so many hundreds of people that were laying there, shot there. So I looked at this, and I started to walk. And I walked away from that grave. I'm going to lay down there? But I walked away then, the grave. And I walked in one direction. I knew approximately-- you see, if I'm going to go there-- I knew the area there. If I'm going to go there, then [STAMMERS]. So I walked, until that's the morning. This was in-- not September, you know? The day was still a big-- long-- until the morning, when it started to get a little light. It started to get a little light.

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw some women coming out from the-- from the-- a house there. So I went over there. They said, oh, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- for sure, from Treblinka. I said, yes. Help me. I say, please. And I started to-- started to cry, say, help me. Said, I'm afraid. I cannot do this. If they're going to see me, I'm-- they're going to kill me. I said I would not go away. They would have to help me. So she took me in, inside, and took a pot with water, and she bent me down, and she washed me [STAMMERS]. And said, give me a-- a clean rag. And I tied it up. And she gave me a piece of bread. And she said, you know, shh. Don't-- run away from here. I don't want to see you. I don't want to hear from you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went away from her. How long can you walk, you see, after being shot, and wandered, and with that blood from the mouth? I walked a few hundred feet away from her, and I came over to the other side from that forest. And I laid down, until I said, well, I'm going to lay down. And if I'm going to wake up, and I'm going to go back to this, I wouldn't know where I  am. So I know-- I-- [STAMMERS]. As a soldier, I knew a little bit, the strategy, you know. I took a-- a piece of wood and put it in front. And I-- I laid down with that hat here. It make up something in the top. If I'm going to wake up, I have to go in that direction where the wood-- the piece of wood is, because it shows me to go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I lay down, and I fell asleep. And I was sleeping there a few hours, this looks like. And I got up, and I started to walk. I was about more-- almost about 14, 16 days there, not having food, not having water, not having where to sleep, and nothing, and swollen. I was swollen. This picture you have here-- this is already after-- after this. And at night, I went into some of the-- in the-- in the-- you know, into houses. And I asked for help, a piece of bread. Some of them, they gave it to me. And some of them, they said, run away. I don't want to look at you. I don't want to see you. I don't want to get killed. Well, maybe they were. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: One evening, I was so hungry, I fell I'm dying. I'm-- I had to have something to my mouth. I didn't know what. Well, first of all, I eated the-- the-- from the field, the corn, this-- the-- you know? The wheat?

speaker: interviewee
text: The wheat. So I ate it. And-- and-- and I was sleeping in that, you know? This was the time when they take it off the-- the-- from the field, the corns with-- so I made a-- a-- a place where to sleep. I opened up the bundle with the corn, and I crawled in and I sat-- I was sleeping there. Now, when I-- I-- I-- I felt I can, I have to have something to eat. I came into a house. And they were sitting-- the whole family was sitting about-- potatoes, and the soup, and everything is-- and that smell from that. I would eat it. And I said, give me. I-- [STAMMERS] give me a-- give me something. No, no. [NON-ENGLISH   SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- as I was standing there, so I ran over to that pot, with both hands in the-- in the-- in-- in the pot, and grabbed the potatoes. And I put them in my mouth. And I [STAMMERS] and I ran around. I felt that I-- I couldn't do anything about. And-- and I still was there in the-- you know, still not a-- then the whole front was-- you know, the shooting was over my head, you know? And I was laying, and I was thinking about that picture with Paul Newman. I'm-- I'm-- [STAMMERS] he runs away from the-- from the prison, you know? And I was-- I saw this picture before the war, you know? And I saw that. I was thinking, oh, I-- I-- I'm look-- I saw this, and I-- and the-- the-- this is almost the same thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one-- from the last-- the last days, until I came into a-- also to a farmer. And I have-- asked him to eat. He said, look, I can give you every day something to eat. Now, you cannot stay here a-- a minute longer. You have to get away from here. If you give me something, let me-- he gave me bread. He gave me a little the tobacco, [INAUDIBLE] because I was smoking. Then he said, tomorrow night, come again. I went back. I had already a  piece of bread. And I-- you know, he gave something-- a little water-- I should have something, you know, to drink. And I went back to mine apartment-- to that straw. I was laying there. And I-- over the day. And the next day, the same thing. One day later, I came in there. He said, son-- told me like that-- son, you are free. The Russians, they're here. You're not going away from here anymore. You're going to stay here. And I-- he gave me  so much to eat. [STAMMERS] He put me in the-- in that attic, I should sleep. I got sick from that what he gave me to eat. In the morning, I get up. He said, now you can go. The Russian Army-- I saw them at-- they are here, about 200, 300 feet away from here. The soldiers are there. I said, no, I'm not going. I'm afraid. [STAMMERS] Might kill-- [STAMMERS] I don't know what's going to happen. If you're going to do me-- if you're going to go over there, and you're going to come back, and you will tell me you just saw them, then I'm going to-- then I'll go. He-- he left me in the house, and he picked up himself, and he went away. 10 minutes later, he came and he went-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Say, I just talked to them. You can go. You can go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he gave me bread. He gave me tobacco. He gave me a little water. And he said, goodbye to me. And I picked up myself, and I started to walk away from him.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I walk away from him-- and I walked to that direction where he told me-- and I came over-- I was afraid. He said-- he-- he-- one thing he told me-- be careful here. This means, if somebody will see you here from the Poles, most probably, you will not get away from here. So be careful. I walked, and I take a look there. I see soldiers who are different [NON-ENGLISH]-- different clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was afraid to go over. I don't know. Well, finally, I said, what can I lose? More than the-- [STAMMERS] this was left for me to live? Die here? What's the difference? So I went over, and I looked at them. And I said, this is not the Deutsch Army-- this is not the German Army. That's the Russian Army. And I picked up my hands, and I started to scream-- yeah! [NON-ENGLISH]! I'm a Jew! I'm a Jew! [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Run away from here, because if you're going to stay here, here's-- here, they're shooting each other. You see? You can get a-- a bullet. [STAMMERS] So I picked up myself, and I started to walk. I walked away from them. And then, I walked. Said, where shall I walk? Said to go to Sokolov. Sokolov. Sokolov has go to be about 10 kilometers. I cannot walk. You see? I feel I'm swollen. I cannot. [STAMMERS] I cannot help myself. I have to go. So I walked.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I'm walking-- and I'm on a main road-- I hear-- I hear a-- a truck. Someone [STAMMERS] drive-- riding somewhere. So I picked up myself, and I stepped in the middle of the road, and picked up my hand. Do whatever you want to me, I-- I cannot-- can't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: He stopped in front of me. And I [STAMMERS] Russian, I know how to say in Russian [SPEAKING  RUSSIAN], I said. [SPEAKING RUSSIAN] So I see a-- an officer jumped out from the car and kicked-- and looked, and looked, and looked at-- and looked at me, and he [SPEAKING  RUSSIAN]? [STAMMERS] I didn't need to tell you more. You help me. He said, what can I help you? I'm going to [INAUDIBLE] from the front. Said, I cannot help you. So  he sat down with me, and said [YIDDISH]-- I'm telling this in Yiddish-- [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. [CRYING] [SPEAKING YIDDISH] Just like I'm crying, he was crying like a baby. He said, [YIDDISH], I cannot do you anything. I have to go to the front.

speaker: interviewee
text: One thing I can do for you-- I'm giving you a-- some paper. If you've got to come-- go to Sokolov. If you're going to come to the road-- to the main road, you will see a lot of soldiers, a lot of transportation there, and a lot of people there-- not civil people, the soldiers. I said goodbye to him. We kissed each other. And I-- and I picked up myself. I started to-- I had to walk another five, six miles, [INAUDIBLE]. And I walked with that stick in my hand. And I walked, and I walked, and I walked. Finally, I came to that corner-- to that-- I made that five, six miles, and I came to that corner with whole-- [STAMMERS] the Russian Army, tanks, and some cars, and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stopped there behind-- by one car. It's not-- was not a car, it was a-- a horse and buggy, you know? There was [INAUDIBLE]. And I stopped for him. And I looked at the man, and I took out that paper, and I show it to them. He looked at me, and he said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]? I was, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Come on. He took me on that wagon. We went in-- we drove them to Sokolov. And we-- they stopped. It looks like they stopped somewhere, because they had to cook some food.

speaker: interviewee
text: He say, you're not going away from here, until we take care of you. You sit here. I sat down. And they sent over-- right away, noises, and--
